methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Seed → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.5x) to $14M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Space & Aerospace (found 0 same-stage, 1 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 23 same-sector peers exist in Space & Aerospace, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

methods.name: Public-Comp Anchoring
methods.skip_reason: target revenue not on file (private companies rarely disclose)
methods.rationale: Public Space & Aerospace P/S median is 59.8x across 6 tickers. Cannot apply without target's revenue.

data.narrative:
Starpath Robotics is a seed-stage Space & Aerospace company founded by Saurav Shroff and Mihir Gondhalekar, having raised $14.5M to date. The sector includes a modest public comp set of five tickers (RKLB, PL, LUNR, ASTS, SATL) and eight private peers spanning the stage spectrum, though no seed-stage peer set exists with sufficient density for reliable median benchmarking.

Given data constraints, only one valuation method is applicable: stage-progression analysis. Using total capital raised ($14M) as a proxy for post-money valuation at seed close, a typical 2.5x step-up multiplier to Series A entry yields an implied valuation of $36M. This reflects standard expansion-stage repricing for companies demonstrating early traction sufficient to progress rounds.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $31M to $42M (median $36M), anchored entirely to stage-progression mechanics. Critical uncertainties remain unresolved: revenue or ARR is unavailable, precluding public-comp anchoring despite a 59.8x median P/S across the sector; the peer set lacks same-stage density, preventing cross-sectional median benchmarking; and burn rate, runway, and unit economics are not on file. Any material deviation from standard seed-to-Series-A progression—whether driven by exceptional technical risk, customer validation, or cap table dynamics—cannot be quantified from available data and would warrant reassessment.
